audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar complaint, shared-spirit, quail, and plague text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Taberah fire, food craving, manna description, Moses' leadership collapse, seventy elders, Eldad and Medad, universal-prophecy wish, quail, and Graves of Craving. Strong burnout, mental-health, anti-authoritarian, and anti-victim-blaming safeguards required.

verses.restored: The people became as complainers, evil in ears of YHWH; YHWH heard, and his anger burned, and fire of YHWH burned among them and consumed at edge of camp.
The people cried to Moses; Moses prayed to YHWH, and fire sank down.
He called name of that place Taberah/Burning, because fire of YHWH burned among them.
The mixed multitude among them craved a craving, and children of Israel also returned and wept, saying, Who will give us flesh to eat?
We remember fish we ate in Egypt for nothing, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic.
But now our soul/life is dried up; there is nothing at all except this manna before our eyes.
Manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance like bdellium.
The people went about and gathered it, ground it in mills or beat it in mortar, cooked it in pots, and made cakes from it; its taste was like taste of cakes with oil.
When dew descended upon camp by night, manna descended upon it.
Moses heard the people weeping by clans, each at entrance of his tent; anger of YHWH burned greatly, and in eyes of Moses it was evil.
Moses said to YHWH, Why have you done evil to your servant, and why have I not found favor in your eyes, that you put burden of all this people upon me?
Did I conceive all this people? Did I give birth to them, that you should say to me, Carry them in your bosom as nursing guardian carries infant, to ground you swore to their fathers?
From where do I have flesh to give all this people? For they weep to me, saying, Give us flesh that we may eat.
I am not able, I alone, to carry all this people, for it is too heavy for me.
If thus you do to me, kill me, please kill me, if I have found favor in your eyes, and let me not see my wretchedness.
YHWH said to Moses, Gather to me seventy men from elders of Israel, whom you know as elders of people and its officers; take them to tent of meeting, and let them stand there with you.
I will come down and speak with you there; I will take from spirit that is upon you and put it upon them, and they shall bear burden of people with you, and you shall not bear it alone.

To people you shall say, Sanctify yourselves for tomorrow and you shall eat flesh, for you wept in ears of YHWH, saying, Who will give us flesh to eat? For it was good for us in Egypt. YHWH will give you flesh, and you shall eat.
Not one day shall you eat, nor two days, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days,

but up to a month of days, until it comes out from your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you, because you rejected YHWH who is in your midst and wept before him, saying, Why did we come out from Egypt?
Moses said, Six hundred thousand on foot are people in whose midst I am, and you have said, Flesh I will give them, and they shall eat a month of days.
Shall flock and herd be slaughtered for them and be enough for them? Shall all fish of sea be gathered for them and be enough for them?
YHWH said to Moses, Is hand of YHWH shortened? Now you shall see whether my word happens to you or not.
Moses went out and spoke words of YHWH to people; he gathered seventy men from elders of people and made them stand around tent.
YHWH came down in cloud and spoke to him; he took from spirit that was upon him and put it upon seventy elders. When spirit rested upon them, they prophesied, but did not continue.
Two men remained in camp; name of one was Eldad and name of second Medad. Spirit rested upon them—they were among those registered but had not gone out to tent—and they prophesied in camp.
A young man ran and told Moses, saying, Eldad and Medad are prophesying in camp.
Joshua son of Nun, attendant of Moses from his youth, answered and said, My lord Moses, restrain them.
Moses said to him, Are you jealous for me? Would that all people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would put his spirit upon them!
Moses withdrew to camp, he and elders of Israel.

A wind went out from YHWH and drove quail from sea and let them fall beside camp, about a day's journey this side and a day's journey that side, around camp, and about two cubits above face of ground.
The people rose all that day and all night and all next day and gathered quail; one gathering least gathered ten homers, and they spread them all around camp.
While flesh was still between their teeth, before it was cut, anger of YHWH burned against people, and YHWH struck people with very great plague.
He called name of that place Kibroth-hattaavah/Graves of Craving, because there they buried people who craved.
From Kibroth-hattaavah people set out to Hazeroth, and they were at Hazeroth.

translation_notes: Moses' statements in verses 11-15 are leadership-collapse and death-wish language. The text does not shame him; the immediate Divine response is shared leadership.
The seventy elders receive spirit so burden is distributed rather than concentrated in one leader.
Eldad and Medad prophesy outside the expected location, and Moses refuses Joshua's attempt to monopolize spiritual authority.
Verse 25's final phrase is textually debated and may mean they did not continue or did not cease; it requires witness audit.
The people's craving must not be reduced to mockery of appetite or cultural food memory. The narrative critiques rejection of liberation and destructive excess.
The plague is ancient judgment narrative and must not be used to blame people for illness, hunger, addiction, or eating behavior.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 11 to shame overwhelmed leaders, people in mental-health crisis, hungry communities, migrants remembering familiar food, or those suffering illness. The chapter's clearest constructive movement is from impossible solitary burden to shared spirit and distributed leadership.
